---
title: "You are what doesn't surprise you"
draft: true
date: 2026-03-09
description: "..."
categories:
---


Consider what it means to be a distinct thing in the world — to be a something rather than nothing, here rather than there, this rather than that. It seems obvious, almost trivially so. But follow the implications carefully and you arrive somewhere unexpected.
If you are a distinct thing, you have a boundary. Something separates what you are from what you are not. And the world on the other side of that boundary does not stand still. It changes — sometimes gradually, sometimes abruptly — in ways that are not entirely your doing and not entirely under your control. Things happen that you did not cause. Events unfold that you did not initiate.
This is not a problem unique to humans or even to living things. It is the basic predicament of any bounded system in a changing world. And it has a consequence that follows with something close to logical necessity: to persist — to continue being the thing you are rather than being overwhelmed, dissolved, or reorganised by events outside you — you must be doing something about this. You must, in some sense, be anticipating what the world will do next.
This is the beginning of knowledge. Not knowledge as a cultural achievement or a philosophical ideal, but knowledge in its most basic functional sense: the capacity of a bounded thing to not be perpetually caught off guard by the world it inhabits.
Now notice what surprise actually is. It is not simply encountering the unfamiliar. When you roll a dice with no expectation of any particular outcome, the result — whatever it is — does not surprise you. You had no model, made no prediction, and so no prediction was violated. Surprise, in the relevant sense, requires a prior expectation. It is what happens when a model you were relying on turns out to be wrong. It is the signal that your internal picture of the world has failed to match what the world actually did.
This distinction matters. Pure ignorance — the blank slate, the absence of any model — is not a state of comfort or safety. It is a state of maximum vulnerability. If you have no model of anything, every event is equally possible and equally unmanageable. The pressure to reduce that openness, to build some structure that allows anticipation, is not optional. It is the pressure of existence itself on any system that has a boundary to maintain.
Watch an infant and you see this process running in its most transparent form. A newborn is surprised by almost everything. But the nervous system is not passive in this — it is already searching for correlations, already building associations between what happens and what tends to follow. When the infant begins to notice that a particular internal signal — a motor command, a proprioceptive trace — reliably co-occurs with the sight of its own hand, the hand starts to lose its power to surprise. The correlation, once established, turns an unpredictable external event into an anticipated consequence of the infant's own action. The hand is absorbed — no longer part of the world that can catch you off guard, but part of the system that does the anticipating.
What has happened here is not merely learning a fact. A new boundary has been drawn. The hand, which was once on the far side — part of the unpredictable world — has been incorporated. And here the insight cuts deeper than it first appears: the self is not a fixed container into which experience flows. It is a process, continuously redrawn along the line of what can and cannot surprise you. You are, quite literally, what doesn't surprise you.
The world beyond that line remains. The infant's mother, the hunger that arrives without warning, the sounds and lights that carry no reliable correlation with anything the infant controls — all of this remains surprising, and therefore other, and therefore the target of ongoing attempts to find patterns, build models, extend the reach of anticipation. Not because the infant has decided to learn, but because this is what a bounded system in a changing world necessarily does. The only alternative is dissolution.
And what are models, exactly? They are not copies of the world. A model is an internal structure that allows you to generate expectations — to say, in effect, given what I know of how things tend to go, here is what I anticipate next. The model is always an approximation. It is always wrong in some respects and useful enough in others. Its purpose is not to represent reality faithfully but to reduce the number of times reality catches you unprepared. Genuine knowledge, in this sense, is not the accumulation of accurate facts but the continuous refinement of anticipatory structures — models that fail, that get corrected, that are rebuilt with greater precision, and that in being rebuilt, reshape the system that holds them.
This argument — that any bounded, self-organising system necessarily acts to minimise the gap between what it expects and what it encounters — turns out to be the conceptual core of one of the most ambitious scientific frameworks of recent decades. The Free Energy Principle, developed by Karl Friston and colleagues, formalises exactly this claim in mathematical terms, applying it not only to brains but to any system that maintains a boundary against a changing environment. I arrived at it here from a philosophical direction rather than a mathematical one. But the destination is the same.
In future posts I want to examine what this means for how we understand ourselves — not just as things that know, but as things that differ from each other in how they know. If the self is continuously redrawn along the line of what surprises us, then individual differences are not fixed properties of fixed objects. They are different solutions to the same problem: how to persist as a something, in a world that never stops changing.

Further reading
The scientific framework in the background here is Karl Friston's Free Energy Principle. His 2010 paper "The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?" in Nature Reviews Neuroscience is the key technical reference. For a more accessible entry point, Andy Clark's Surfing Uncertainty (2016) develops the predictive processing framework that shares this foundation. The philosophical tradition this argument touches includes Kant's analysis of the conditions of possible experience and, more recently, the relational approaches to quantum mechanics developed by Carlo Rovelli — both of which turn on the question of what any knowing system must be like, rather than what the world is like independently of any knower.
